title: Modi's First Ethiopia Visit: India Courts Global South Leadership Platform
lede: Prime Minister Narendra Modi arrived in Addis Ababa for his first bilateral visit to Ethiopia, signaling New Delhi's strategic commitment to strengthening partnerships within the Global South.

sections.context: Prime Minister Modi was invited by Ethiopian Prime Minister Dr. Abiy Ahmed Ali for this maiden bilateral visit to the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia, arriving in Addis Ababa on December 16, 2025. The Indian Ministry of External Affairs framed the visit as a demonstration of India's commitment to deepening ties with Ethiopia specifically and the broader Global South community. The timing and framing suggest India is actively positioning itself within networks of developing nations, with Ethiopia—as the headquarters of the African Union and a continental power—serving as a critical node in this strategy. The MEA delegation included Secretary (ER) Sudhakar Dalela, Ambassador to Ethiopia Anil Rai, and Joint Secretary (E&SA) Janesh Kain, underscoring the diplomatic weight India attached to the engagement.
sections.threads: India's engagement with Ethiopia follows a pattern of high-level bilateral visits aimed at consolidating India's presence in strategically important regions and institutions. Similar diplomatic initiatives have characterized India's approach to other key Global South partners, as seen in [[india-mm-myanmar-presidents-india-visit-signals-strengthened-str-2026|Myanmar's strengthened strategic engagement]] and broader efforts to build coalitions within developing economies. Ethiopia's position as the seat of the African Union adds particular significance—this visit positions India within African multilateral forums at a time when India is competing for influence within the Global South bloc. The MEA's emphasis on strengthening partnerships "for the larger community in the Global South" indicates this is not transactional diplomacy but rather foundational work for a broader coalition-building strategy.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor the outcomes of this visit for concrete deliverables: bilateral agreements, frameworks for cooperation, and any statements on multilateral coordination within African or Global South institutions. The next 6-18 months will reveal whether this visit catalyzes substantive economic, diplomatic, or security partnerships or remains primarily symbolic. Key decision points include whether India pursues joint positions with Ethiopia on African Union matters, trade agreements, or development cooperation frameworks. Watch also for whether this visit generates follow-on engagement with other African powers and whether Ethiopia becomes a platform for India's broader African strategy, particularly in competition with other Global South powers for continental influence.
